title: Catechist Guide — The Contemplative Tradition
html:
Catechist Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Class Summary

Estimated Duration: 60 minutes

Central Theme: The three expressions of prayer (vocal, meditation, contemplative) and the contemplative tradition as the vocation of every baptized person.

Objectives:

  • Distinguish the three expressions of prayer: vocal, meditation, and contemplation.
  • Understand what contemplative prayer is and that it is within reach of all the baptized.
  • Know and practice the method of lectio divina in its four steps.
  • Learn to face distractions, spiritual dryness, and the dark night.
  • Discover the richness of the contemplative tradition (Teresa of Ávila, John of the Cross, Desert Fathers, Taizé, Hakuna).

Key Verse: «Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things, but one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion» (Lk 10:41-42)

Catechism References: CCC 2700-2724

Scripture Passages: Lk 10:38-42; 1 Thes 5:17; 1 Kgs 19:11-13; Ps 46:11; Mt 6:6; Ps 119:148

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration Catechist Actions
1. Big Question 8 min Tell St. Teresa's story: 18 years of difficult prayer. Ask: «Can you pray without words?» For younger groups, open with Martha and Mary.
2. Three expressions of prayer 12 min Explain vocal prayer, meditation, and contemplation. Use concrete examples. Vocal prayer with pauses activity (Hail Mary).
3. Lectio divina 10 min Explain the four steps with Psalm 131. Do a mini lectio divina in class (5 min in silence).
4. Martha and Mary 8 min Read Lk 10:38-42 aloud. Discuss: what does it teach us about the action-contemplation balance?
5. Difficulties in prayer 7 min Distractions, dryness, dark night. Normalize them: they are not failure. Briefly tell John of the Cross's dark night.
6. For Reflection 7 min Share in pairs or triads. Then gather some responses as a group.
7. Closing: Key Idea + Cultural Richness 5 min Read the Key Idea aloud. Highlight a cultural resource (Fra Angelico, Taizé, Hakuna). Closing prayer.
8. Activity for the week 3 min Hand out workbook: guided lectio divina with Psalm 131, practice of God's presence, the silence challenge.

Notes for the Catechist

St. Teresa as the Gateway

Teresa's story is the best argument for this class. Many students feel like failures in prayer. Knowing that a Doctor of the Church spent eighteen years getting distracted and wishing prayer time would end is liberating. Tell it with warmth: Teresa was funny, wrote with humor, cooked well, was deeply human. Don't present her as an unreachable saint from the distant past. If your group is children, Martha and Mary is a better entry point than Teresa.

«Contemplation is only for mystics» — FALSE

This is the most persistent and harmful myth. The Catechism repeats tirelessly that all the faithful are called to contemplative prayer (CCC 2714). Actively demolish this from the beginning. Contemplation is not ecstasies or visions: it is the simple gaze of love. A peasant of Ars summed it up gazing at the Tabernacle: «I look at Him and He looks at me.» Anyone can do that. You don't need to be a theologian or a monk.

Silence makes people uncomfortable — and that's normal

Many people are afraid of silence. In our culture, noise is constant and silence is perceived as emptiness or failure. Don't force long silences in class. Start with one minute, then two, then three. Explain that discomfort in silence reveals our addiction to noise: that is exactly why we need to cultivate it. If a student becomes distressed with silence, let them breathe with eyes open while looking at a sacred image. There is no single correct way.

The dark night is not depression

Be very careful here. St. John of the Cross describes a spiritual purification in which God withdraws sensible consolations to purify love. It is not a pathological experience or clinical depression. If a student describes symptoms of depression (inability to enjoy anything, insomnia, thoughts of death, withdrawal), do not diagnose it as the dark night: refer them to professional help. The dark night is distinguished by the desire for God that persists despite the darkness.

The catechist needs personal experience

You cannot teach contemplation if you have never tried to pray in silence. You don't need to be a mystic or an expert on Teresa of Ávila. But you do need to have had some encounter with the silence of prayer. Your brief, honest testimony («I struggle too, but I've discovered that…») will be more convincing than any explanation from the Catechism. Don't be afraid to share your struggle: it humanizes the class.

Lectio divina in class: practical guide

Before class, choose a short passage for a mini lectio divina in the classroom. Recommended: Psalm 131 (only three verses). Or blind Bartimaeus (Mk 10:46-52). Announce the steps in a soft voice: «Now we read… now we meditate in silence… now we respond to God… now we simply rest in Him.» Five minutes is enough. Don't expect spectacular results. The goal is for them to try the method, not to have a mystical experience.

Music for contemplation in class

Having soft instrumental music during moments of silence can greatly help those who feel uncomfortable. Options: Taizé instrumental, soft Gregorian chants, Hakuna piano, even pure silence if the group can handle it. The music should not be the star: it is accompaniment that helps lower defenses.

Age-Specific Adaptations

🌱 Seed (children 7-12)

Focus: Martha and Mary. Prayer as listening to Jesus.

  • Use the image of Martha and Mary as two friends of Jesus. One cooks, the other listens.
  • Activity: everyone seated in a circle, eyes closed, one minute of silence. Ask: «What did you feel? Did you hear anything?»
  • Don't use words like «contemplation» or «dark night.» Talk about «being with Jesus in silence,» «listening to Jesus.»
  • Concrete activity: teach them to say «Jesus, I love You» silently, very slowly, before going to sleep.
  • For younger children, use an image of Jesus and ask them to gaze at it in silence for one minute.

🌿 Sprouts (youth 13-17)

Focus: Contemplation = holy rebellion against digital saturation.

  • Connect with their world: noise of notifications, FOMO (fear of missing out). Contemplation as a space of freedom.
  • Teresa's story connects powerfully: a young woman who got distracted in prayer just as they get distracted by mental TikTok.
  • Present lectio divina as an alternative to infinite scrolling: instead of consuming others' content, listening to God.
  • Hakuna and Taizé are resources they likely know or might find interesting.
  • Activity: three minutes of silence with phone off and face-up on the table. If they can, have them repeat it.
  • Discussion question: «Can you be someone's friend without spending time alone with them? Then how can you be Jesus' friend without prayer time?»

🪴 Root (adults new to the faith)

Focus: Contemplation as the natural goal of Christian life, starting from scratch.

  • Many adults think «praying is asking for things.» Expand the horizon: praying is being with God, and contemplation is the purest form of that being.
  • Present lectio divina as a concrete method for those who «don't know where to start» with the Bible.
  • Normalize difficulty: use the gym analogy. No one runs a marathon on day one. The same goes for prayer.
  • Encourage them to start with five minutes daily. Without expectations. Without measuring results. Just show up.
  • The «practice of the presence of God» by Charles de Foucauld may feel very accessible: it doesn't require extra time or a special place.

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Focus: The deep Carmelite tradition and the integration of action and contemplation.

  • Go deeper into Teresa of Ávila (Interior Castle, seven dwelling places) and John of the Cross (night of the senses vs. night of the spirit).
  • Explore the distinction between acquired contemplation (human effort aided by grace) and infused contemplation (purely gratuitous gift from God).
  • Apophatic mysticism: The Cloud of Unknowing and the tradition of Pseudo-Dionysius. Contemplating God as the Wholly Other, beyond all concepts.
  • Theological-spiritual debate: «Can you be contemplative in the midst of the world without withdrawing to the desert?» Explore secular vocations of the Carmelite Order.
  • Ecumenical connections: the Jesus Prayer (Orthodox hesychasm), Zen and Christian contemplation according to Thomas Merton.
  • Recommended reading: Living Flame of Love by St. John of the Cross, The Way of a Pilgrim, New Seeds of Contemplation by Thomas Merton.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 493-509 (Expressions of prayer, contemplation).
  • Youth Catechism: Section on contemplative prayer.
  • Sacred Scripture: Psalm 131; 1 Kings 19:11-13; Luke 10:38-42; Matthew 6:6.
  • St. Teresa of Ávila: Life (autobiography), The Way of Perfection, The Interior Castle (freely available online).
  • St. John of the Cross: Dark Night of the Soul, Spiritual Canticle, Living Flame of Love.
  • Thomas Merton: New Seeds of Contemplation.
  • Charles de Foucauld: Writings on the presence of God.
  • Henri Nouwen: The Way of the Heart (contemplation in daily life).
  • Anonymous: The Way of a Pilgrim (the Jesus Prayer).
  • Anonymous: The Cloud of Unknowing (English apophatic mysticism, 14th c.).
  • Film: Into Great Silence (2005) by Philip Gröning — the life of the Carthusians. Essential viewing.
  • Film: Of Gods and Men (2010) — Trappist monks, contemplation and martyrdom.
  • Art: The Annunciation by Fra Angelico (Convent of San Marco, Florence) — perfect image of contemplation.
  • Art: The Ecstasy of St. Teresa by Bernini (Santa Maria della Vittoria, Rome).
  • Music: Hakuna Group Music (especially «My Center,» «Simply,» «Hurricane»).
  • Music: Taizé (meditative chants).
  • Music: Gregorian chant (for moments of classroom silence).
  • Web: www.taize.fr — chants, resources, and youth gatherings.
  • Web: www.hakunagroupmusic.com — songs and resources.

Difficult Questions

«How do I know if I'm contemplating or just thinking about my stuff?»

Contemplation is not measured by the absence of thoughts (that's impossible for most people). It is recognized by its fruits (cf. Gal 5:22-23): peace, patience, love, humility, compassion. If after your times of silent prayer you are a bit more patient with your family, a bit less irritable, a bit more able to forgive — that is a sure sign God is at work. The criterion is not what you feel during prayer, but how you live afterwards. St. Teresa insisted on this: consolations in prayer are a gift, but virtues are the proof.

«What if I fall asleep?»

No worries! Falling asleep while praying is probably the best way to fall asleep. St. John Vianney, the Curé of Ars, saw a peasant who came to the church every day and sat before the Tabernacle. He asked what he was doing. The peasant answered: «I look at Him and He looks at me.» If in that looking you fall asleep — does a father take offense because his child falls asleep in his arms? On the contrary: it is the greatest sign of trust. Falling asleep praying is resting in God. Only if it happens every single time, try praying at a moment when you are more awake.

«Is contemplation compatible with a busy, active life?»

Absolutely. The great revolution of the modern Carmel was to prove that you can be contemplative in the middle of the world. Charles de Foucauld was a hermit in the Sahara but lived among the Tuareg. Teresa of Calcutta spent hours before the Tabernacle and then plunged into the streets of Calcutta. Pope Francis speaks of «contemplatives in action.» The key is the order: first be with God, then act. Action flows from contemplation as a river flows from its source. If we cut the source, the river dries up even though we keep running. A busy life is not an excuse to abandon prayer; on the contrary, it is the reason we need it more.

«What is the difference between Christian meditation and Eastern meditation?»

Similar in technique, radically different in content. Eastern meditation seeks to empty the mind to achieve inner peace or the dissolution of the self. Christian meditation seeks to fill the heart with the presence of a personal God who loves us. In the Eastern tradition, silence is sought as the goal; in the Christian tradition, silence is the means for encounter with Someone. Christian contemplation does not dissolve us into the whole; it leads us to union of love with God without losing our personal identity. Put another way: we don't meditate to become nothing, but to become more fully ourselves — children in the Son. That is why Christian contemplation always ends in love of neighbor, not isolation.

«St. John of the Cross talks about 'emptiness' and 'detachment.' Isn't that like Buddhism?»

The resemblance is superficial. In Buddhism, emptiness is ontological (ultimate reality is void and the self is an illusion). In St. John of the Cross, emptiness is pedagogical: we empty the soul of disordered attachments so that God can fill it with Himself. We don't empty ourselves to become nothing, but so that God becomes everything in us. It's like cleaning a glass: you don't clean it to leave it empty, but to fill it with the best wine. The goal of Christian detachment is not nothingness, but transforming union: «the soul becomes one with God through love.» God does not annihilate us: He plenifies us. Merton devoted much effort to explaining this difference.

«How do I distinguish the dark night from a crisis of faith or depression?»

Three key distinctions:

  1. The dark night produces desire for God. Even though you feel nothing, you still want to be with Him and do His will. In a crisis of faith, you doubt whether God exists or is worth it. In depression, you desire nothing.
  2. The dark night produces virtue. Though you suffer, you are more patient, more humble, more understanding toward others. Depression usually produces irritability, withdrawal, and deterioration of relationships.
  3. The dark night is temporary and cyclical. It comes in stages, and God gives moments of light to sustain you. Clinical depression is persistent and does not improve through prayer alone; it needs treatment.

If someone talks to you about spiritual darkness, listen carefully. Refer them to a priest or a professional depending on the symptoms.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord, You who taught Teresa to find You
not in the heights but in simple friendship,
teach me as well.
I am no mystic, I am no saint,
I am a catechist who wants to speak of You
without betraying Your mystery.
Give me the right words,
and above all, give me the right silence.
May what I teach not be theory,
but fruit of what You have worked in me.
And may my students not learn about prayer,
but begin to live prayer.
In the gentleness of Your Spirit. Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
